title: Copperplate connected script
subtitle: Building a flowing pointed-pen script with consistent slant and pressure variation
excerpt: Copperplate is a pointed-pen script in which thick and thin strokes are produced by varying pressure on a flexible nib, not by changing nib angle. The letters are fully joined and written at a consistent forward slant. This tutorial covers the essential oval, the compound curve, and the ten letters that build from those two movements.
sourceNotes: Michael Sull, Learning to Write Spencerian Script (1991, Mardel Publishing). Also draws on nineteenth-century copy books from the Spencerian tradition (public domain via the HathiTrust). The oval and compound curve drill described here follows Sull's method.

glossaryTerms.definition: A downstroke made with increased pressure on a flexible pointed nib, causing the two tines of the nib to separate and lay down a wider, darker mark. The shade is the thick part of a Copperplate letter.
The thinnest stroke possible with a pointed nib: made with the lightest possible pressure, using only the nib tip. Hairlines are used for upstrokes, entry strokes, and joins between shade strokes.
The consistent forward lean of Copperplate letterforms, conventionally set at 52° from the horizontal baseline. All main strokes and ovals share this slant.

content.text: Copperplate differs from broad-edge calligraphy in the mechanism of variation: thick strokes come from pressure that separates the nib tines (the
), and thin strokes come from feather-light contact (the
). All main strokes lean at the same
of 52°. Controlling the pressure transition smoothly is the advanced skill; this tutorial begins with the two foundational shapes.
Prepare the paper and set the slant grid

Copperplate requires very smooth, non-absorbent paper: 220 gsm cartridge or hot-press watercolour. On rough paper the nib catches in the fibres and splays. Rule a guide sheet with baseline and x-height lines, plus a grid of 52° slant lines spaced 4 mm apart. Tape the guide sheet under a fresh practice sheet on the light box.

Draw a straight downstroke on the slant line: begin with no pressure at the top, gradually increase pressure through the middle of the stroke, then release pressure again by the baseline. The stroke will be thin at the top, widest in the middle, and thin at the base. This is the fundamental Copperplate pressure change. Repeat across the page until every stroke looks identical.

The Copperplate oval is slanted at 52°, taller than wide, and has one shade-stroke side (the left-to-bottom sweep) and one hairline side (the right-to-top return). Begin at 2 o'clock on the oval: hairline clockwise over the top, shade down the left side, hairline back up the right, and close at the start point. The shade should be on the down-left diagonal only.

The oval and shade stroke combine to build: a, d, g, q (oval-based letters); o (a pure oval closed at top); n, m, h, u (compound curve: a hairline up, shade down, hairline up over the arch, shade down the second leg); i (single shade stroke with a hairline lead-in). Write 'noma' and 'unidad' repeatedly before attempting other letters. All joins are hairlines.

attrs.body: The Nikko G is the right starting nib for Copperplate because it is stiff enough to control. The Hunt 101 listed as optional has a much finer tip and more flex, move to it only once the pressure drill is consistent. Using the Hunt 101 too early leads to ink splats, snagging, and inconsistent shades.
